hints.text: Plate: black w/ **black dot** in middle (**AR only**), or white w/ blue top bar (blue bar also Brazil)
Gen3 **black car** (S. America: only AR/PE/UY); smallcam exists (AR/PE/BR)
**White-and-red chevrons** (only country in the Americas with these)
Round concrete poles (3 lines, alternating) + doubled poles; **wooden A-frame poles** (only AR in S. America)
km markers white w/ black top strip: **RN** = national, **RP** = provincial road
Default vibe: flat, treeless, sparsely populated, "wide open" & desolate
Far **west** mountainous (Andes); east rarely has mountains
**Red soil → Misiones** (Brazil-like); Chaco/Formosa = sandy soil + low bushes
**Cardón cactus** (candelabra) → NW mountains (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca, Tucumán)
Tierra del Fuego: flat grass + beech forest; Neuquén = flat w/ single poplar rows
Pampas grass → Pampas; sugarcane → Tucumán
Vineyards + mountains west + flat east → **Mendoza** wine region
Salt pans → Jujuy; red bricks (Bolivia-like) → NW (western Jujuy)
**Eucalyptus (gum) trees** everywhere, light bark; drives on the **left**
Bollard: white, **red reflector front / grey back** (NZ = red wraps around the back)
Almost all **white** road lines — yellow lines → South Africa or NZ instead
Give-way sign: white border + **black** text (NZ = red text); speed sign red circle on white rectangle (NZ = no rectangle)
Gen3 = long antenna; chevrons white-on-black & black-on-yellow
**Stobie pole** (steel + concrete) = **South Australia** (iconic)
Rusty metal poles w/ holes = **Northern Territory**; 130 km/h speed limit = NT only
**Yellow signposts = Western Australia**; ~64% of concrete poles → Victoria
**Red 'No Stopping' signs = NSW only** (others white)
'C' roads only in Victoria & Tasmania; fluorescent-border road signs → Queensland
Darwin (NT): dark red soil, black tree trunks; termite mounds → NT
Tasmania: cool temperate green rolling hills (like NZ/S. Chile)
Coastal N. Queensland tropical: palms + sugarcane; inland NSW flat, redder soil further inland
Bus-stop designs unique per state (Darwin = concrete shelters; Tasmania say 'metro')
Snorkel rare cars → NSW/ACT (2 in WA); Christmas I. & Cocos count as Australia for streaks
Plate: white + **blue top stripe** (since 2018); red = commercial
**Portuguese**: -ão (not -ón), -nh (not -ñ), -lh (not -ll)
**Red soil** across most of the country (not NE, not most of Rio Grande do Sul)
"Ladder" poles: rectangular, small holes near top (also Paraguay)
Road-sign backs painted **black** (also Paraguay)
Chevrons **yellow-on-black** (rest of S. America uses black-on-yellow)
Roads: double yellow centre + white outer lines; white painted kerbs common
Transparent satellite dishes (unique in Latin America); blue Fortlev roof water tanks
Gen3 car: white, stubby antenna w/ diagonal coil (also EC/CO/MX); smallcam exists (only BR/PE/AR in S. America)
Coverage mostly eastern half; Amazon very sparse
NE = Caatinga: dry soil, shrubs, succulents
Paraná pines (araucaria, flat-top branches) → south: PR, SC, RS
Carnaúba palms (fan leaves, spiked lower trunk) → CE, PI, RN
Area codes (DDD) grouped by 1st digit; ending in **1** = state capital
2-letter state acronyms on km markers / state road numbers
CEP postcode first 2 digits: numbering runs anti-clockwise from São Paulo → ends Rio Grande do Sul
Round concrete poles → south (esp. São Paulo); wooden poles → RS, Roraima, Amazonas, RJ
Roraima: open grassy plains, distant mountains, thin bunched grass
"Brown mushroom" insulators → only Pernambuco; "white mushroom" → Bahia
"Pencil pole" (smooth cone top) → Rio de Janeiro; pole street-names painted → Paraná
Speed signs say **"MAXIMUM"** (US = "SPEED LIMIT"); km/h, higher numbers
British English (centre, colour) + metric; less wordy signs than US
**Diamond-shaped T-intersection sign = Canada only** (US uses rectangular)
Usually **single** yellow centre line (US = more double yellow)
Lots of **Gen2** (rural/remote) → if Gen2 in N. America, likely Canada; Gen3 mirror = two peaks (US single)
Canada Post **red** logo; communal mailboxes (rare in US, USPS = blue)
**Alberta bollard**: cylindrical, 2 black bands (white/yellow between); very common at intersections
**BC bollard**: thin, slightly curved, black+white (esp. Vancouver Island)
**NWT bollard**: cylindrical w/ 2 *thick* black bands (AB bands are thinner)
Manitoba: black+grey bollard w/ orange stripe
**French majority → Québec** + parts of New Brunswick; "Arrêt" stop sign = QC only; "Stop/Arrêt" bilingual = NB
Québec: white guardrail bollards w/ red/green reflector; yellow fire-hydrant markers on signposts
Ontario: km markers w/ white square at bottom (unique); diamond roadside markers
Saskatchewan: alternating pole-top insulators (1L/2R then 2L/1R); white triangles on posts
BC: green sign backsides; PEI/Nova Scotia transformers mounted at 45°
**Alberta rural roads numbered sequentially** (Twp Rd / Range Rd) → near-exact location from a sign
Mainland = **tripod coverage** only (motion blur, saturated colours), no car (HK & Macau have car coverage)
**Simplified** Chinese characters (Traditional → Taiwan/HK/Macau)
Plates **blue with white text**
Coverage = small spots near east-coast cities (Beijing, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Chengdu, Yangzhou); much is inside museums / ancient cities
Plate 1st character = province indicator (京 Beijing, 沪 Shanghai, 粤 Guangdong…) — learnable
Forbidden City / Palace Museum = central Beijing; Zhoucun = only pinpointable ancient city (straight N–S street)
Museum tells the city: Hubei (Wuhan, palace grey roofs), Suzhou (white/grey angular), Long Museum (Shanghai, boxy concrete)
Plates: long white, **blue strips both sides** (left more visible) — Italy has equal strips + shorter front plate
French: é à è â ê î ô û, œ, ç; **-ux** suffix only French; street = **rue**
**French bollard**: round white, **pointed top**, reflector band all around (Scotland's is blunt-topped)
**Waffle/ladder poles** (step indents; also Spain) + small **blue plates** on poles (France-unique) + diamond "French poletops"
**Yellow "D" road-number signs** = France only; town-entry signs red border w/ thin white outline
Chevrons **white-on-blue** (only France + Spain; Spain uses only 2 or 4 arrows)
Middle-line dashes = **two offset blocks** → France; blue house numbers (Italy rarely blue)
Peugeot / Renault / Citroën ≈ half of all cars; smallcam (most of W. Europe)
**Postal code first 2 digits = department number** (alphabetical); also inside the right blue plate strip
Phone: **01 = Île-de-France**, 02–05 run clockwise from the NW
**Department logos** on bus stops / km markers / signbacks → identify department
Stone houses, gable roofs w/ orange tiles (S) or black slate (N); very diverse regional architecture
Regional languages appear (Breton NW, Alsatian E, Occitan/Basque/Catalan S)
All car coverage = **Gen4**; **bollard roughly every 50 m** (B&W, white/grey reflectors, orange at intersections)
German: **Ä Ö Ü ß**; Luxembourg has near-identical bollards but **3 bolts** (Germany = 2)
**Yellow town-entry signs** (black text, metal frame) = town / municipality / county
**"Einbahnstraße"** one-way sign = Germany (Austria = "EINBAHN"); yellow mailboxes
km markers: **blue = Autobahn (A-road)**, white w/ black border = B/local; black/dark sign backs (also IT/RO/AL)
**Wind turbines** far more common than anywhere else in Europe; green windshield sticker ("4")
Elevation: NW very flat · centre hilly · **south = Alps** (Austrian border)
**Postal codes counter-clockwise: 0 = Saxony (E) → 9 = N. Bavaria**
Place-name suffixes: **-ow / -itz = east** (Slavic); **-weiler / -ach = south**
"Commie blocks" (socialist-era apartments) → former **East** Germany
Brick houses → NW; thatched roofs + rock walls → Schleswig-Holstein / N. Frisian islands; vineyards → SW
Bavaria: white 3-section road markers + round wooden bollards; black-disk bollard bases → all of eastern Germany
Roadside post styles differ by state (rocky unpainted → Rhineland-Palatinate/Saxony; tall white → Saarland/Thuringia)
Mostly **"shitcam"** (brownish colours, big circular blur); Gen4 = smallcam; drives on the **left**
Plates: long, usually **white**; commercial = **yellow**, EV = **green**
**B&W striped signposts** (also BD/NP/LK); poles almost always concrete/metal (wood v. rare); common = square concrete pole w/ trident top
English on much signage; many regional scripts (best region tool → below)
**Devanagari** (bar across top of words) = Hindi → central/north; Marathi (has ळ) → Maharashtra
**Gujarati** = like Hindi but **no top bar** → Gujarat
**Bengali** (left-facing triangles) → West Bengal/NE (& Bangladesh); Assamese variant (ৰ ৱ) → Assam
**Kannada** (curves + small circles) → Karnataka; **Telugu** (checkmark diacritics on top) → Telangana/Andhra
**Tamil** (curves + 90° angles) → Tamil Nadu (& Sri Lanka); **Malayalam** (upside-down U shapes) → Kerala
**Oriya** (round "skull-emoji" tops) → Odisha; **Punjabi** (broken top bar, soft letters) → Punjab
Meghalaya = almost exclusively **English**
**Holey concrete poles** = Gujarat; yellow tuk-tuks w/ black roofs → south (TG/AP/TN)
Pole paint: **5+ thin** black/yellow stripes → Delhi; **exactly 4 thick** → Bihar
Gurdwaras (white domes, Khanda symbol) → Punjab; bright flat orange ridged roof tiles → Tamil Nadu
Plates **black w/ white letters** (3 white sections through blur); commercial = yellow; newer = white (Malaysia = 2 sections)
Drives on the **left**; **motorcycles have front plates** (unique in SE Asia)
**Red-and-white banners** everywhere; cigarette ads (word **PERINGATAN**, ~18,000 Rp)
Yellow **waystones** angled toward road w/ cutout naming 3 towns (Philippines = perpendicular, text away)
Language Dutch-influenced: **televisi** (Malay = televisyen); currency Rupiah (Rp)
**No coverage on Papua**; sparse Kalimantan/Aceh/Central Sulawesi
**Kabupaten (Kab.)** regency names on signs → pinpoint region (learnable)
Postal codes: **1xxxx = Jakarta → 9xxxx = Maluku/Papua** (west→east)
Satellite-dish angle → which side of equator + how far (points to W. Kalimantan)
Oil palm → Sumatra/Kalimantan; rice → Java; Nusa (Lesser Sunda) drier & shrubbier
**Bali**: crown-like roof pinnacles, Hindu temples, dark andesite stone; blue horizontal pole text
Cardinal words in addresses: Utara(N) Timur(E) Selatan(S) Barat(W) Tengah(C)
Red soil w/ white-soil layer spilling on road → Bangka Belitung islands
Plate: two blue strips + **short front plate** (unique; Albania has 2 strips but not short)
**Triangular** bollard, white w/ black top, red front / white rear (Albania identical)
Road lines often = white outer + **no middle line**; **double guardrails** common w/ red reflectors (Spain = yellow)
Italian: double consonants, lots of i's, words end in vowels; street = **via**
Round concrete poles (unusual for the Med) + concrete trident poletops; black/dark sign backs
km markers: white square, **Roman numeral (100 m) over Arabic (km)**; "passo carrabile" no-parking signs
Road codes **SS / SR / SP** = State / Regional / Provincial; chevrons white-on-black
Area-code first digit generally increases **east then south**
German bilingual signs → South Tyrol; French ("via/rue") → Aosta Valley; Slovene → near Slovenia border
**Po Valley** = dead flat + hazy air (north); Bari = olive groves + red dirt + rock walls (SE)
Sicily = arid hilly + rocky mtns; Sardinia = grey rocks poking up (NE); cacti → far south
**Trulli** (conical stone roofs) → Itria Valley / Alberobello; Tuscany = irregular brown-grey brick
Volcanoes as landmarks: **Etna** → E Sicily, **Vesuvius** → Naples; Venice = canals everywhere
**Low-cam** (camera looks down, road looks wide) — only other always-low-cam = Switzerland (blurred there)
Drives **left**; writing = kanji + hiragana (loopy, soft) + katakana (geometric, loan words)
Plates: short white w/ **green text**; Kei cars = **yellow w/ black text**
Round concrete poles w/ screw-like extensions + info plates (region key); road lines **never yellow on the outside**
Prefecture roads = **hexagonal shields** (kanji under the number); distinctive red inverted-triangle stop sign; traffic mirrors; rural retaining walls
**Area codes increase toward the south**
Learn place-name kanji: 県 (prefecture), 市 (city), 北/南/東/西 (N/S/E/W) → fast city finding
4 main islands N→S: Hokkaidō, Honshū, Shikoku, Kyūshū; 47 prefectures
Pole plates, reflectors, guy-wires & transformer mounting styles differ per region (Hokkaidō, Tōhoku, Kantō, Kansai…) — strong pinpoint tools
Orange arrow above top pole plate → Chūgoku; orange/black pole bands → Shikoku
Hokkaidō = wide flat grid farmland, snow; subtropical dense green → Kyūshū/Okinawa
Bollards may have the city/prefecture written in kanji → near-pinpoint
**Octagonal concrete poles** (also Colombia); short plates, front plate required
Small **white cylindrical bollards w/ black base** = Mexico-unique; white outer + single yellow middle lines
Stop sign **"ALTO"** (S. America + PR = "PARE"; also PA/CR/GT use ALTO)
Cacti everywhere; thick arrows on signs; yellow accents on kerbs; rooftop cylindrical water tanks
**OXXO** & **Pemex** stations; Nahuatl place names (lots of x, tl, -tlan, -ec)
Has **Gen2** (rare in Latin America); Gen3 white car w/ stubby coiled antenna; US pickups common
Area-code 1st digit = region; postal codes ordered alphabetically by state name
**Blue agave fields → Jalisco/Nayarit** (tequila); Mexican Giant Cardon cactus → Baja California
Saguaro cactus → Sonora; **boojum trees** (tentacle-like) → southern Baja California
Monterrey = tall mountains ringing the city; blinding **white soil → Yucatán** (Quintana Roo)
Red brick buildings → states west of CDMX (Jalisco, Michoacán, Guanajuato)
**Jalisco bollards** have crown + "Jalisco" text (only Mexican bollard with writing)
Bollards w/ red band near top → Nayarit; thick cross on sign backs → Chihuahua
Plates long **yellow** + blue strip left (Europe yellow = only NL, Luxembourg, UK-back)
**Essentially no street-level utility poles** (Belgium has them); extraordinarily **flat** land
Water everywhere: canals, ditches, dikes, bridges; windmills (old & wind turbines)
**Bike paths everywhere** (reddish-pink lanes) + tons of cyclists; brick roads common
Brick **rumble strip** on rural road edges (~unique); speed bumps w/ long/short white stripes
Street signs **blue w/ white text**; street = straat (-weg, -laan, -plein); Dutch double vowels, ij/oe, "IJ" one letter
Road markers **green**: **A-road = red box** (highway), **N-road = yellow box** (regional); sign edges wrap to the back
Landline area codes (3–4 digit) are geographically grouped → region
N-road **hectometre markers** have province-specific designs → identify province
**Frisian** language on some signs → Friesland (north); low hills → S. Limburg / De Veluwe (rare)
Row houses ≈ half of housing (very high); red-brown brick, orange/grey tiles
Plates **fully white, black text** (Europe usu. has blue strip)
Cyrillic w/ **Ы Э Ё Ъ**; улица = street (ул.), город = city (г.)
Many bollards + unusually **wide intersections**
Square concrete poles (also post-Soviet states); concrete support blocks at pole base
B&W striped guardrails / kerbs / pole shields
Gen3: black or white car w/ **long antenna**; pedestrian crossings = alternating white+yellow stripes
Birch everywhere; **larch** more common further east (Siberia)
Kamchatka = grey volcanic soil + volcanoes; Caucasus mtns → south (Dagestan dry/flat)
RHD Japanese cars more common **east of Novosibirsk**
Area codes: **8-** = west, **3-** = central, **4-** = east/Moscow
Gen3 unblurred plate regional code (right side): 01 = Adygea … learnable list
Omsk = birch patches in green farm fields (summer Gen4); Kaliningrad = white-striped lined oaks
Bilingual signs (Tatar, Bashkir…) → Tatarstan / Bashkortostan
Plate: long white, single **blue strip left** (Portugal = yellow strip right; Italy = two blue strips)
Bollard: yellow-orange front reflector + **two white dots on the back**, hollow
**Hunting signs** ("coto…caza" or black/white diagonal split) = Spain-unique; lots of high-voltage pylons
Pedestrian crossing = **8 stripes** (most in Europe; else only Andorra)
Red sign borders run **to the edge** (France/Italy/Portugal keep a white edge); flat signposts
Ladder poles + French poletops; chevrons black/white or **blue/white** (blue = only Spain + France)
Spanish: **Ñ** (vs Portuguese ç/lh/-ão); street = **calle**; garage no-parking = "Vado permanente"
**Regional road codes** on markers (SG = Segovia, etc.) → pinpoint province
Area codes: 2 digits → very accurate; all landlines start with **9**
**Catalan** (à, ç, "i" for and, street = carrer, "Gual permanent") → Catalonia / Valencia / Balearics
Basque (x/k/tx heavy, non-Romance) → Basque Country/Navarre; Galician → NW (Galicia)
Diverse: dry Mediterranean plateaus & hills common; lush Atlantic green → far N (Galicia/Asturias)
Rural roads may have **dashed outer lines** (helps vs Portugal/Italy where they're absent)
Plates: **white front, yellow back** (Ireland = white both + always a blue strip); drives **left**
Middle line = long dashes with **cat's-eye reflectors** in the gaps; double-yellow no-stopping lines at kerbs
UK bollard: rounded, red-front/white-back reflector, black base & top w/ white stripe
Chevrons **black w/ white arrows** (Ireland = yellow arrows); **triangular** red warning signs (Ireland = diamond yellow)
**"GIVE WAY"** yield (Ireland = "YIELD"); pole warning sticker shows a **man being zapped** (Ireland = no man)
Roads: **A / M** = major, **B** = minor; terraced / semi-detached houses (61%); brick + chimney pots
A/B-road **first digit = zone** (grouped, like a pie); NI phone = **028**, London = **020**
**Wales**: bilingual English/Welsh signs; road marking **"Araf"** (= Slow) is Wales-only; Welsh uses LL, W, Y
**Scotland**: Scottish Gaelic (green/yellow text) north of Glasgow; barren mountainous **Highlands**; 'Passing Place' diamond signs; red+white round bollards
Scotland stone: granite → Aberdeen, red sandstone → Glasgow/Dumfries, yellow sandstone → Edinburgh
England countryside = green, hedgerows, low rolling hills (Ireland looks similar — check other clues)
Warning stickers wrapped all the way around the pole → Northern Ireland; Cornish palms → far SW coast
Isle of Man = **Gen2 only**, counts as UK for country streaks
Speed signs say **"SPEED LIMIT"** (Canada = "MAXIMUM"); **miles**, feet/inches
Wordy signs (railway crossing spelled out); US English (center, color)
Yellow centre + white outer lines; **double yellow** common (v. rare in Europe)
White+orange fibre-optic markers ~unique to US; rumble strips common
Trailer/mobile homes rural (esp. SE); Baptist churches common
"No Passing Zone" yellow triangle = US only
US+Canada: post mailbox w/ flag; wooden poles + cylindrical transformers; short plates (no blue strip); overhead signal bars; long-nose semis (Europe = cabover)
Gen2 rare → if Gen2 in N. America likely **Canada** (except Alaska, some Hawaii); Gen3 mirror = single peak (Canada = two peaks)
East of Missouri R. = denser pop. & greener; West = mountains, drier
Deep South (OK→SC) reddish soil; SW Four Corners red mesas/tablelands
PNW (WA/OR/N.CA coast) temperate rainforest; Great Plains flat, few trees
New England architecture (wood panel, porch, high roof) → north
Interstates: **even = W–E, odd = S–N**; lowest numbers south & west
~half of states have **no front plate**; each state has a unique plate design
3-digit area codes (not clustered but learnable); state flags appear
Appalachians (low forested mtns) → east; Rockies → MT–NM; Great Basin (mtns + dry plains) → NV
